title: The Exhaustion of Trying Harder
body:
Most Christians I know are tired. Not tired of their faith — tired in it. Tired of the gap between what they know they should be and what they are. Tired of the cycle: resolve, effort, failure, shame, repeat. Tired of a version of the Christian life that functions primarily as a performance improvement programme.

The strange thing is that this is not what the New Testament describes. Paul's letter to the Galatians is a sustained argument against exactly this mode of operating. 'Are you so foolish?' he asks. 'Having begun by the Spirit, are you now being perfected by the flesh?' He is talking to people who started well and then, under pressure, reverted to self-effort. He considers this a serious theological error, not just a motivational problem.

title: What 'Christ in You' Means in Practice
body:
'Christ in you, the hope of glory.' The phrase appears in Colossians and is one of the most compressed and consequential claims in the New Testament. It is not a metaphor for moral aspiration or the inspiring memory of Jesus. It is a statement about indwelling — about the actual presence of the risen Christ in the life of the believer.

What this means in practice is harder to articulate. The experience of it varies enormously. But the consistent testimony across the Christian tradition is that it involves a gradual shift from 'I am trying to live as Christ would' to 'Christ is living in and through me.' The difference is not semantic. One is self-powered. The other is not.

title: Grace That Is Actually Free
body:
Dietrich Bonhoeffer's distinction between cheap grace and costly grace has been widely discussed, but there is a related error he does not name that is equally common: grace that is theoretically free but functionally conditional. We believe, doctrinally, that salvation is by grace through faith. But in practice we treat our standing before God as dependent on how well we are performing.

This is not a minor inconsistency. It produces the exhausted Christianity I described in the previous post. If grace is actually free — if God's posture toward the believer genuinely does not fluctuate based on performance — then the Christian life becomes something entirely different. Not licence, but rest. Not passivity, but freedom from the driving anxiety of 'am I enough?'

title: Why Spiritual Disciplines Can Become Their Own Trap
body:
Prayer, fasting, Bible reading, sabbath — the classic spiritual disciplines are good. But they can be practised in a way that keeps the self-powered mode fully intact. If I read my Bible in order to feel that I have met a standard, or pray as a transaction ('I have done my part; now You do Yours'), the form is correct but the orientation is wrong.

Dallas Willard made a distinction that has been useful to me: disciplines are not performances to be evaluated but training for a life. The goal is not the discipline itself but what the discipline makes possible — a more receptive, more attentive, more genuinely available orientation to God. When the discipline becomes the point, something has gone wrong.

title: Rest as Theological Statement
body:
The Sabbath is the only commandment in the Decalogue that is grounded in creation rather than ethics. God rested on the seventh day — not because He was tired, but because rest is built into the structure of time itself. The human failure to rest is therefore not merely a health issue. It is, in some sense, a failure to live within the reality that God has made.

For those whose Christianity has become primarily about effort and achievement, the Sabbath is a weekly subversion of that mode. You are commanded, literally commanded, to stop. To receive rather than produce. To trust that the world will continue without your management of it for twenty-four hours. This turns out to be very difficult, which tells you something.

title: When Prayer Feels Like Talking to the Ceiling
body:
Almost every Christian I have spoken to honestly about prayer has described seasons when it felt entirely hollow. The words went up and nothing came back. The practice that once felt alive became a duty, then a guilt, then something avoided. This is not a fringe experience. It is, by the testimony of the saints and mystics, nearly universal.

What the contemplative tradition offers at this point is not a technique but a reframing. The felt absence of God in prayer is not evidence that God is absent. John of the Cross and Teresa of Ávila both describe seasons of spiritual dryness as occasions for a deepening of faith — for learning to trust in what you cannot feel. This is cold comfort in the middle of it. It is more useful in retrospect.

title: The Difference Between Guilt and Conviction
body:
Guilt and conviction can feel similar from the inside, but they function very differently. Guilt, in the theological sense, is backward-looking and self-focused: I am bad, I have failed, I am not enough. It produces shame and withdrawal. Conviction is forward-looking and Christ-focused: this is not consistent with who I am in Christ, and change is possible. It produces movement.

The pastoral damage done by conflating the two is considerable. Christians who live primarily in guilt are not living in the reality of grace. They are living in a performance dynamic with added shame. The gospel addresses this directly — not by lowering the standard but by changing the question from 'am I good enough?' to 'am I available?'

title: What It Means to Abide
body:
'Abide in me and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit by itself, unless it abides in the vine, neither can you, unless you abide in me.' The vine and branches image in John 15 is Jesus's own description of the mechanism of the Christian life. The branch does not produce fruit by effort. It produces fruit by remaining connected.

This is such a simple image that its radicalism is easy to miss. The branch has one job: stay attached. The life, the growth, the fruit — these come from the vine. The branch's role is receptivity, not production. If this is the model Jesus intended, it suggests that much of what passes for Christian activity is the branch trying to produce fruit through its own resources, detached from the source.
